items.myth: A non-significant result (p > 0.05) proves there is no effect.
items.reality: You may lack power to detect a real but small effect. Report confidence intervals and sample size; "not significant" means insufficient evidence, not confirmation of zero.
items.example: n=20 per arm, true lift 2%: p might be 0.4 while a 95% CI on the difference spans −1% to 5%—compatible with both null and meaningful improvement.

items.myth: Smaller p-values mean larger or more important effects.
items.reality: p-values combine effect size and sample size. A tiny effect with n=10,000 can yield p ≈ 0.0001; a large effect with n=8 may not reach α.
items.example: Mean latency drops 0.5 ms with n=50,000 → p ≈ 0.001 (significant but trivial); drops 50 ms with n=12 → p ≈ 0.08 (not significant but operationally huge).

items.myth: If p = 0.06 and α = 0.05, the result is "almost significant" and H₀ is basically false.
items.reality: The α threshold is arbitrary; p = 0.06 is not qualitatively different from p = 0.04 without context. Report exact p-values, CIs, and pre-registered α.
items.example: Two replications get p = 0.04 and p = 0.07 for the same effect size—together they describe uncertainty better than treating 0.05 as a cliff.

items.myth: Type II error rate β is fixed once you choose α.
items.reality: β depends on sample size, effect size, variability, and test type. Power = 1 − β must be planned; otherwise you risk underpowered studies.
items.example: With α=0.05, n=25, and σ=20, detecting a true mean shift of 5 gives power ≈ 0.35—β ≈ 0.65, so most real effects are missed.
